title: FRAD087_052-Histoire d'Antoine Malaty | Story of Antoine Malaty
text:
Story told by Mr. Pierre Dudognon, grandson of Antoine Malaty. Antoine Malaty, born in Sauvagnas (Lot-et-Garonne), was a career soldier (policeman on horseback). He wrote to his wife and children during the Great War several cards that partially retrace his journey. Several of these maps represent Champagne and the North of France. Intoxicated by a mustard gas attack, he was repatriated to the rear: he was at the beginning of 1918 in Saint-Denis, in the barracks of the 110th Gendarmerie section. From December 1918, he was in Rouen, from where he sent new cards to his family. Antoine Malaty's correspondence bears witness to his concern for his wife, living with her children on her parents' farm. Little said about his living conditions, he evokes his prospects for leave, and some political events, such as the attack perpetrated against Georges Clémenceau in 1919. Antoine Malaty continued his career in the gendarmerie after the war. He was awarded the 1914-1918 Interallied Medal and the 1914-1918 War Commemorative Medal. Ernest Durand and Pierre Dudognon, respectively maternal and paternal uncles of Mr. Pierre Dudognon, also participated in the First World War.

- photograph of Antoine Malaty dressed as a mounted policeman (1910) - correspondence exchanged by Antoine Malaty with his family (1915, 1918-1919) - inter-allied victory medal (with diploma) and commemorative medal of the 1914 war- 1918

title: FRBNBU-103 Georges Kohn photographe des " chevaliers du ciel " | Documents from Beilin family
text:
Georges Kohn (great-great-uncle of contributor Jacques Beilin) from Central France, an architect after the war, was hired as an aviation photographer for reconnaissance missions during the conflict. This collection of 184 photographs is a reflection of the war as Georges Kohn was able to see it during his reconnaissance missions. We can see in particular combat zones, battlefields riddled with shells, Nieuport planes, Farman planes, squadron insignia, flying aces, destroyed monuments, Amiens, Craonne, the coast 108, attacks seen from the sky, the Italian zone, ground planes, military airfields, a German armored tank, etc.

184 aerial and terrestrial photographs, large and small format.

title: FRAD087_007-Correspondance et carnet d'Alphonse Faure | Story of Alphonse Faure
text:
History of Alphonse Faure told by his granddaughter. Born in Limoges in 1878, Alphonse Faure did not fight the whole of the war, which he started in the North of France. He was incorporated into the 89th Territorial Regiment, 4th Battalion, 14th Company. It passed in particular during the conflict in the Vosges, in Lorraine and in the Somme. During his presence at the front, Alphonse Faure sent many postcards to his wife Louise and his two daughters. The first are views of the towns and regions he passed through; as the conflict progressed, he sent more and more views depicting the destruction caused by war. He evokes in his correspondence his daily life, the cold, or the parcels sent to him by his family. It was probably his wife who put together all of these maps after the war in an album. This also brings together cards addressed to the Faure family by friends, as well as cards sent by the daughters of Alphonse Faure to their father during the conflict. Alphonse Faure also kept, from August 16, 1914 to November 26, 1915, a war diary in which he described very precisely his journeys and his daily life at the front.

Album of postcards exchanged between Alphonse Faure, his family and his friends; Alphonse Faure's war diary; fighter card

title: FRAD067-123/2 Second album de Nicolas Hiessler (1884-1952), infirmier en Flandre et en Artois (armée allemande) | Album from Nicolas Hiessler, with photographs from the Western Front
text:
It is in memory of Miss Valentine Hiessler (born in Oberbronn on May 31, 1914, deceased), that she was still going to visit in 2006 at the retirement home of Haguenau, which Mrs. Teresa Weller entrusted to the Departmental Archives of Bas- Rhine two photographic albums from the father of this acquaintance, Constable Nicolas Hiessler (31/10/1884-13/05/1952). Consultation of this man's individual Veteran's file (Arch. dep. Bas-Rhin, call number 844 D 165) makes it possible to retrace his career and corroborate dates and places captioning his war photographs. Private, non-commissioned officer (03/28/1915), then nurse-sergeant (01/20/1918), Nicolas Hiessler served from August 3, 1914 to November 22, 1918, essentially (from October 31, 1914) in the Bayerische Sanitäts Kraftwagen Abteilung 6. As such he took part in position combat in Flanders and Artois from October 14, 1915 to June 23, 1916 and from January 1, 1917 to April 8, 1918 - services which he still in 1946, difficulties in asserting as justifying his rights to retirement as a combatant. He also took part in the fighting to defend the border in Lorraine (08-19/08/1914, within the 42. inf. Div. (XXI AK)), in the battles near La Bassée, Arras (09/05 -23/07 and 25/09-13/10/1915) and Armentières (09-18/04/1918); he ended the war on the western front, in Flanders and still in Artois (19/04-06/09/1918). Holder of the “Mil. Fuhrerschein 3 b”, Nicolas Hiessler was decorated twice: Bay. mil. Green. Kreuz 3. Kl. md Kr. u. Schw. (27/11/1916); Prof. Eis. Kreuz 2. Klasse (07/10/1918). The two photographic albums he kept from the war attest to his passage, between 1916 and 1918, in Brussels, Valenciennes, Anzin, Arras, Hénin-Liétard (today Hénin-Beaumont), Croizilles, Hunes, Winkles, Loos-en -Gohelle, Lens, Loison-sous-Lens, Pont-à-Vendin, Vimy, Douchy-les-Mines, Mouchy, Roubaix, Ostend, Tournai, Bailleul, Herlies, Raismes… See also contribution FRAD067-123/1.

2 - Photographic album, approx. 28 x 21cm, 1916-1918

title: FRAD067-120 Regrets d'un membre du peloton d'exécution, Villers-en-Haye (Meurthe-et-Moselle), 7 février 1915 | Postcard about an execution
text:
Taken from a rich collection of patriotic postcards, an anonymous card attracts attention by its content more than by its motif. It bears the text, unsigned: "Villers-en-Haye, Meurthe-et-Moselle, February 7, 1915. Today I have a moment to let you know some news from the country, which is not the most brilliant; but, what do you want, we have hope, we are pushing back the Boches day by day; yesterday we just made a deal that did not suit me – when you are ordered, you have to do it – we shot a married man from the 100th who had deliberately injured himself on October 27; he passed the court martial on the 5th, condemned to death, shot by the 50th, the poor devil did not suffer; that the same thing does not happen again, I will do my best not to find myself there.

Post card

text:
Story of Jean Collet told by his grandson. Jean Collet was a sharecropper in Saint-Auvent (Haute-Vienne), a profession he continued to exercise after the Great War. Little is known of his career during the war, except that he belonged to the 89th territorial infantry regiment and that he fought throughout the war, especially around Verdun. He received the 1914-1918 War Medal and the Inter-Allied Victory Medal.

Commemorative diploma of the First World War to which are attached the 1914-1918 war medal and the inter-allied victory medal awarded to Jean Collet.

text:
A photo album with motifs from both the western and mainly the eastern front came from Florence Payne from Bremen. The motifs show views of places on Russian territory, now Polish territory (including Ostrowo, Kolno), war scenes and groups of soldiers and soldiers who are not known in detail. The objects also include a photo of a soldier who probably belonged to the 1st Sailor Artillery Battalion from Friedrichsort and a signed photo of an airman from the Artillery Air Battalion (AFA) 220.

Photo album with motifs from the western front (from France) and from the eastern front (Russia).

title: AMSM-002_2 : Correspondance à mon grand oncle Auguste PIERREQUIN | correspondence of my grand uncle Auguste PIERREQUIN
text:
After buying the house of my uncle, Auguste PIERREQUIN, I found this correspondence addressed to his parents, Alphonse and Hortense, sent by a cousin, Edmond BIGOUDOT The latter went to war, on the western front and was wounded in combat on September 12, 1918. In a first letter of August 31, 1916, he was worried about the bombardments suffered by the family and mentioned his life, particularly behind the front (relations with officers, food, vaccination). He also gives his feelings about Romania's recent involvement in the fighting. The rest of the correspondence consists of postcards. The first was sent on September 5, 1919, in which he evoked his convalescence in Moret. The particularity is that it was he himself, during his free time, who drew this postcard in the name of great uncle Alphonse. The second, dated September 17, 1919, is accompanied by 3 other postcards which have remained blank, on which are inscribed the names of the other men of the family.

Letter and postcards.

title: FRAD067-119 Cartes reçues de Wilhelm Keller (1873-1920) (armée allemande) par son épouse Salomé Eberhardt (1874-1955), à Bischheim (Bas-Rhin) | Postcards from Wilhelm Keller
text:
Plasterer, married in 1904 to Salomé Eberhardt (1874-1955) and father of two children, Alfred Wilhelm (1907-1991) and Magdalena Salomea (1905-1985), Mme Keller-Gatty's grandfather, Wilhelm Keller (1873- 1920), died as a result of the war. In the absence of an individual veteran's file in the Departmental Archives of Bas-Rhin, or of personal military papers, the cards he sent to his wife from 1914 to 1917 are perhaps the only source making it possible to trace his eventful journey, across the whole of Europe, during his war years. W. Keller (photograph at the top of the series of images) was first engaged on the Western front, in Belgium: he wrote from Enghien (26/11, 14 and 19/12/1914; 12/01, 11 /02 and 06/04/1915) and occasionally from Jemappes (08/12/1914). He continued the war on the eastern front, between Poland and Hungary: he dated his 4 successive maps of May 1915 from Kreuzburg, in Upper Silesia (today Kluczbork, Poland), "Hungary", "Galicia" (today Poland/ Ukraine) and Budapest. He then reports to 1. Kompagnie 1. Landsturm Infanterie Ersatz Bataillon Straßburg, XV AK On 08/14/1915, he is in Russia, at Christmas 1915, in Silesia (now Poland), in [Bielsko]-Biala, visibly in military hospital. W. Keller returned to Alsace at the beginning of 1916: his maps of 15/02 and 14/04/1916 are dated from the military camp of Oberhoffen-sur-Moder, near Haguenau, or 05/06/1916 from Karlsruhe (with mention of parking in Oberhoffen). However, he writes on 04/20/1916 from Wlodawa (Poland) and on 06/11/1916 from Parchim (Mecklenburg-Western Pomerania). On this last date, he belongs to the 2. Kompagnie 4. Landsturm Infanterie Ersatz Battalion XV AK 8. Such mobility seems to be explained by the fact that he was assigned to the "Zug Armee" (mention appearing on several maps). An important ellipse no longer allows us to follow his route so precisely after the spring of 1916: the last of the 20 maps that have come down to us – including one undated [1915?], unlocated [Bielsko-Biala?], incomplete [2nd part of a correspondence in 2 cards], placed at the back – is dated from Saarbrücken, 08/09/1917. W. Keller is stationed there injured or ill, giving the address “Res. Lazareth 1. Saarbrücken, Heilstätte Sonnenberg, Zimmer 2”. The front of the cards offers views of Enghien (civil hospital), Brussels (Porte de Hal, Houses of Tailors and Victor Hugo, Royal Theater of La Monnaie), La Hulpe (Castle of Nieuwenhuys), Nieuport (Bridge of the Cup Gorge), Vereshegy (Hungary), the Tatra chain (Poland/Slovakia), Budapest, Oberhoffen-sur-Moder (views of the military camp), Wlodawa, Karlsruhe, Parchim in Mecklenburg. Three other cards (rejected after those of W. Keller), sent by military post to the Keller family in Bischheim, provide specific information on relatives or acquaintances, Georg Daab (21/02/1916, prisoner in Saint-Rambert- sur-Loire (Allier)), Alfred Elgass (17/08/1916, at Res. Laz. III in Hanau am Main (Hesse)) and Konrad Oehl (06/02/1917, 3. Kompagnie 1. Ersatz Regiment 136 at Strasbourg).

24 postcards (all parts preserved)

title: FRBMBO-053 Histoire du soldat Alphonse Robine | Alphonse Robine's story
text:
Madame Nicole Robine bears witness to the memory of her father-in-law Alphonse Robine, a fighter in the Great War. He was in the signals and had on him, during the war, a sketchbook on which he drew daily. He took over this notebook after the war to turn it into a remarkable watercolor album. His album bears witness to the different faces of war. Daily life in the trenches, images of the council of war, Easter ceremony... He wrote to his wife every day. He was a lawyer in Cherbourg.

Main document. Selected extracts from a 164-page watercolor album covering the period 1915-1917.

title: Album zur Erinnerung an Hermann Stegmayer mit Fotos aus Galizien und Frankreich | Album with photos of Hermann Stegmayer, his deployments to France (Vosges, Laon) and Galicia (today: Poland, Ukraine) and his grave in Crécy sur Serre, assembled by his niece.
text: Hermann Stegmayer (born 1891, died 1918) was the contestant's mother's favorite uncle. She collected his letters from him and compiled a photo album of pictures to commemorate him. He was first deployed in France in the Vosges, then came to Galicia (today: Poland / Ukraine), after the December 1917 armistice with Russia, he was back in France where he died. She commented on the album by hand, for example with the entries: "To commemorate my unforgettable uncle Hermann, who died a heroic death on Easter Monday, April 1, 1918 / Offenburg 1914 / for recovery at home in May (?) 1914 / memory to the 3rd I R. 84 / April 15, 1915 Vosges / September 1915 Tête du Violu / Vaccination against typhus May 1916 / Fort Witry, May 1917 / Galicia June 1917 / Kociubincziki (eastern Galicia) – September 21, 1917 / With Russians (at) Armistice in East Galicia December 1917 / Laon March 1918 / Hermann's grave April 3, 1918" / Also included in the album: Death notice: Lieutenant Hermann Stegmayer, leader of the 5th company of an infantry regiment, Iron Cross 1st and 2nd class , and the Zähringer lion with swords / detailed newspaper report about his wounding on March 25th and his death / he died near Crécy s. Serre as Lt. i.e. R. 5./ Replace IR 29.

title: FRAD087_062-"Notes de voyage" de Martial Daigueperse | Diary and documents from Martial Daigueperse
text:
Story of Martial Daigueperse told by his granddaughter. Martial Daigueperse was the only son of a coachman living in Saint-Germain-les-Belles (Haute-Vienne) and a servant born in Bujaleuf (Haute-Vienne). He learned the profession of typographer and worked in Limoges at the Bontemps printing works, specializing in scientific works. He worked there while on leave. During the war, he was a gunner serving in the 34th Artillery Regiment. The war saw him pass through Champagne, and he fought in particular at Verdun, without being injured. Martial Daigueperse kept a diary, written in pencil on two notebooks, which he called "Notes de voyage". This diary covers the period 1914-1919, from his departure from Limoges until his return from the front. He observes daily life at the front, adopting a very neutral tone. He describes the construction of the shelters, the cooking he often does for the group to which he belongs, the German attacks, the news he receives, the permissions, the climate and his fatigue, the attacks to retake Fleury and Thiaumont. Martial Daigueperse died around 1967. His notebooks were fully transcribed by his granddaughter.

Two notebooks entitled "Notes de voyage" (August 7, 1914-March 26, 1919); military booklet; photograph by Martial Daigueperse taken before the war; his parents' family record book.

title: FRAM - Albert et Charles Rauch | Documents from Albert and Charles Rauch
text:
Mr. RAUCH entrusted us with documents relating to his grandfather, Albert Rauch and his great uncle, Charles Rauch, Albert's brother. Their father, Auguste, who lived in SAVERNE (ZABERN) left Alsace in 1880 at the age of 20 after this region became German in 1873 (not to do his military service in Germany). he worked as a locksmith in NANCY, EPINAL, GRANDRUPT, VARVILLE until 1890, then moved to Le Havre (did he want to emigrate to the Americas?). he married Marie RABECQ on July 30, 1892 under German nationality (sic). Charles was born in 1893 and is German like his parents. The family will be naturalized French on March 19, 1894. The 2nd son Albert is born French in 1895. Auguste RAUCH opens a locksmith at 236 route de Normandie in Le Havre (now avenue Aristide Briand since 1932) which still continues its activity today and remains managed by the great grandsons of Auguste. During the First World War, Albert and Charles were both mobilized from August 14. Charles was taken prisoner in Germany on September 14, 1914 and did not return until the end of the conflict. Documents such as postcards, song books and above all a booklet of 38 copied letters are available as well as a censored letter (see the documents). Albert left on September 9, 1915, as a miner in the 21st Engineer Regiment. He was cited in the orders of the 3rd Division of the 1st Colonial Army Corps on July 25, 1918 for acts of bravery, in particular during the battle of the Chemin des Dames (this distinction is the subject of an article in the newspaper " le petit Havre" of Monday, August 12, 1918. After the Armistice, he was assigned to the French occupation troops in Germany and was demobilized in Fécamp in September 1919. Albert received the war cross and then multiple distinctions and diplomas in the part of his cultural life in Le Havre after the Great War Marius CHIBRAN, a soldier and friend of Albert and Charles Rauch, left 2 military instruction booklets on firearms and in particular an extremely detailed rifle through numerous sketches colored.

title: FRAD067-208 Le "petit gas" René Rodier demande pardon à son père (1915) | The little René apologizes to his father
text:
The surname and first name are too common for the little René Rodier, probably born around 1910, to have been able to identify, especially since this is an isolated postcard within a vast thematic collection. . We bet that the posting of the military registers of the classes concerned by the First World War will soon make it possible to remedy this, by providing, to begin with, details on his father Auguste Rodier, on the date of December 8, 1915 corporal in the "70th territorial, 4th Company, 4th Labor Battalion". As it stands, the forgiveness requested, in a still hesitant writing, by the child from his father, shows in a particularly sensitive way the moments of discouragement of these mothers who were sometimes left alone to educate their children with authority.

title: FRAN-PI-38 Histoire du volontaire Marcel Jean Eugène Migot | Marcel Migot' story
text:
House painter, Marcel Jean Eugène Migot is the son of Victor Edouard Migot and Clémence Anne Fougerol. Married to Germaine Berthe Filon, he has a daughter, Nicole Migot. Called on April 12, 1915 in Vannes (Meucon camp), he volunteered to join the front with the 116th infantry regiment, within the 29th Company. He was hospitalized twice: the first time, in Toulouse, from September 16 to December 20, 1916, following a concussion, and the second time, in Chateaudun, for a wound made in the thorax, when he fought at the Chemin des Ladies. Although he was seriously injured, he nevertheless returned to the front in 1918, once his convalescence was complete. His courage is rewarded with numerous decorations: Legion of Honor, military medal, war cross with palms (2 citations), combatant's cross, Verdun medal, Saint-Mihiel medal, medal of the Survivors of Aisne, medal of the wounded, silver and bronze medal of the voluntary combatants. After his return from the front, he wrote a lot about the war. Some of his texts are kept in the municipal archives of Saint-Denis. Others have been published, in particular Le Ravin de Veaudoine (in: Journal of the voluntary combatants). He also participated in the collective work Verdun: history of the fights which were fought from 1914 to 1918, on both banks of the Meuse, directed by Jacques Péricard and published in 1933.

title: Sammlung von Feldpostkarten, Fotos und Dokumenten verschiedener Personen | Collection of Fieldpost, photos and documents of different persons
text:
Field postcards, photos, war propaganda postcards and other documents of unknown origin. Most of the correspondence comes from the brothers Ernst and Hermann Büscher from the years 1914 and 1915. Both of them either inform each other about their condition or they write to their mother in Gelsenkirchen. Ernst Büscher writes to his brother, Landwehrmann Hermann Büscher, from a military hospital in Wachenheim in the Palatinate that he was "injured by a grazing shot on the right shoulder blade 10 km from Longwy" on August 24, 1914. Ernst was then stationed as a hunter in Hirschberg in Silesia, including with the 2nd company of the 2nd Reserve Battalion Jäger No. 5, and was released on June 1, 1918 as Oberjäger. Hermann Büscher was also on the French western front with the 7th reserve company of the 56th Infantry Regiment.

Numerous photos, documents and field postcards, including a certificate of award for wearing the Iron Cross 2nd Class of Corporal Borchert dated May 15, 1918.

title: FRBMBX-058 - Fernand Caron, le courage d'un soldat ordinaire | Fernand Caron, a brave simple soldier
text:
Fernand Caron enthusiastically opens his campaign diary on August 3, 1914. He writes regularly, his story becomes like a daily ritual. A curious isolated double page in the notebook announces his will, written on August 15, 1914. Few pages are filled in this notebook: Fernand Caron sees his brother killed on September 6, the same day he is wounded in the arm and stomach. He died as a result of his injuries on November 29, 1914. His family only had more information when a certain R. Watel, a friend from the Heilbronn hospital, sent them the meager possessions of the deceased. In this sober and moving letter, Watel recounts the last days of Fernand Caron. We read there both the solidarity of his comrades, and the courage of Fernand Caron whom Watel describes as "admirable".

- Campaign notebook: first entry - Campaign notebook: testament - Letter from R. Watel to the family of Fernand Caron (17/04/1919). Account of hospitalization and death.

title: Gefreiter Fritz Düngel, Bursche von Major Wilhelm von Ditfurth | Fritz Düngel under the command of Major Wilhelm von Ditfurth
text:
The recruit Fritz Düngel from Oberhausen served 1912-1914 with the 5th Lorraine Infantry Regiment No. 144 in Metz. In the course of mobilization, his regiment became part of the newly created 33rd Division. The private became the personal assistant to staff officer Major Wilhelm von Ditfurth and followed him to various staffs during the war: Major Wilhelm von Ditfurth was chief of the management department (Ia) on the staff of the 33rd Reserve Division (including in Billy), on the general staff of the Army High Command II (AOK II) in St. Quentin and later in Le Cateau and in 1918 at the General Staff of the IX. Army Corps in Marchè. The album with photos from Metz and the various locations also contains field postcards to "his young lady" Gertrud Dötsch in Oberhausen, to his father Friedrich Düngel in Oberhausen and messages from his "friend Heinrich".

Album with field postcards and photos by Fritz Düngel. Depicted in the photos are, inter alia, Major Wilhelm v. Ditfurth, Grand Duke of Baden at the troop parade in the Billy Gorge on July 7th, 1915, Major vd Busche, several unnamed generals and the locations: Metz, Billy, Combres, Havrincourt Castle, Longeville, St. Quentin, Le Cateau.

title: Ledermappe mit Fotos und Dokumenten von Wilhelm Große Munkenbeck | Leathercase with photos and documents of Wilhelm Große Munkenbeck
text:
Wilhelm (Willy) Große Munkenbeck from Bochum-Stiepel (August 23, 1890 - April 23, 1968) probably studied economics in London before the war. It is not known whether he volunteered for military service or was drafted. He was stationed as vice sergeant of the Bavarian Foot Artillery Battery No. 281 in the Lille/France area and later as a pilot in the 1st Royal Bavarian Flieger-Ersatzabteilung 1 in Schweissheim/Bavaria, where he probably stayed until 1918, as did his pilot's license , issued on February 16, 1918. His tasks included above all reconnaissance flights, on which he made numerous aerial photographs of militarily important areas on the western front, especially in France. Wilhelm regularly wrote postcards to his family in Bochum, and at one point during the war, as the family history relates, he found an opportunity to visit his parents in the Ruhr area. During a plane transfer, he threw the following message over the family home in Stiepel: "I'll be having coffee soon" and landed his plane in a nearby stubble field. After the coffee break, the plane was brought back into position with the help of a team of horses and Wilhelm Große Munkenbeck flew back to Munich. During one of his missions, he was wounded and sustained a bullet wound in his leg, but otherwise survived the war physically unharmed. The Grosse Munkenbeck family worked in agriculture. From 1951 until it was closed down in 1965, Wilhelm Große Munkenbeck operated the small colliery "Mit Gott dared" in Bochum. The bundle includes a collection of weapons, numerous photos, medals and uniform parts as well as other documents.

title: FRBMAN-012 Antoine Laurenty | Antoine Laurenty's story
text:
Antoine Laurenty, a Belgian citizen residing in Brussels, was in his fifties when the Great War broke out. He refused to leave his town and remained there throughout the conflict, despite the dangers and hardships. He began on August 1, 1914 a diary in which he related until his forces abandoned him in 1917 the daily life in the rear, made up of rumors and fleeting hopes. He regularly mentions his nephew René Sibenaler who is fighting in the trenches of the Yser. An edition of this journal was produced by Jean Sibenaler in 2003 at Cheminements editions in Angers under the title "The notebooks of a Belgian citizen, 1914-1918". This book is kept at the Municipal Library of Angers in the collections of the Printer's Legal Deposit under the symbol DLE B54292.

title: FRBMAN-013-Les deux frères François et Jean-Baptiste Moreau | Two brothers, François and Jean-Baptiste Moreau
text:
François and Jean-Baptiste Moreau were two brothers from Saint-Martin-du-Bois (Maine-et-Loire) who were both settled as nurserymen in Guernsey where they were married and each had three children. They were called up together, leaving Angers on September 3, 1914, with the 135th infantry regiment. They were both killed on September 27, 1914, in Prosnes (Marne). Their family inherited a two-handed war diary (François is on the left page, Jean-Baptiste on the right page) copied into a small notebook. This is probably a copy of two notebooks that belonged to each of the two brothers, because it is written by the same hand, and at certain times, the brothers having been separated, they could not write it together; but it is a fine tribute to their parallel destinies. The end of the notebook coincides with their deaths; but they had had the foreknowledge of their approaching death, for the last page contains Francis's farewell to his loved ones. They would be buried in Sillery, near Reims.

title: Souvenirs et notes du cuirassier Jacques PAVARD | Memories and notes from cuirassier Jacques PAVARD
text:
War memories of my great-grandfather Jacques Pavard (29-12-1890 / 02-02-1959, native of Dourdan in Essonne Jacques PAVARD did his military service in 1910. Not having his school certificate, he was put in the section of the illiterate until the day when an officer, entering the barracks, saw him writing the letters of his friends. He was then transferred to the regiment of cuirassiers residing in Cambrai. Returning to Dourdan, the service finished , he resumed his life and his work in the family butcher's shop until August 3, when the tocsin sounded the famous war and when he returned to the 12th regiment of the cuirassiers of Rambouillet.This regiment was attached to the 6th brigade of cuirassiers of Saint Germain of the 7th cavalry division of Melun. In 1917, this regiment passed from the 7th to the 2nd cavalry division then in January 1918, it will become the 12th regiment of cuirassiers on foot. During the war, he never wanted quitting his job as kitchen manager and ravi pruning. Faced with the desire of grandchildren and great-grandchildren to read the notebooks, I decided to transcribe them and publish a book for the whole family in 2009. This testimony may also be of interest to enthusiasts of this period. We meet in particular Lieutenant Destouches who is none other than Louis-Ferdinand Céline. The notebooks relate with modesty his days from September 1914 to April 1919 outside the periods of leave.

Transcription of the war diaries with insertion of some photos, postcards and documents found with the diaries.

Only these last two pieces have not been reproduced for their anecdotal nature or in anticipation of the online distribution of the military registration registers of the classes that took part in the Great War. The notebooks were digitized by extracts: 26 first letters of the 1st notebook, documenting the first 4 months of war, and fragment of diary of the summer of 1915 copied at the top of the 2nd notebook.

title: FRAD078-05 Le décès du capitaine Edmond Rousseau | Death of Edmond Rousseau
text:
The contributor is the grandson of Captain Edmond Rousseau. His story was told to him by his mother. The author of the letters is the captain himself, who died during the war. The photo was taken around 1902. " Edmond Rousseau was Captain in the 34th Colonial Infantry Regiment (based in Toulon), born April 17, 1872 in Boulogne-sur-Mer, he married January 23, 1902, to Madeleine Huret. In 1914 he had two daughters, Gabrielle 12 and Suzanne 8. He was mobilized from the first day, and left on Sunday August 2, 1914. In his hasty departure, he forgot his kepi (which he file), but urgently asks that the photos of Madeleine, Gaby and Suzon be sent to him in Toulon, whom he is very irritated at not having taken with him. In Paris, Aunt Marie Piriou, a cousin of Madeleine, l accompanies him to the Gare de Lyon, and stays with him until the train leaves at 2 p.m.

title: FRAM - Journal de guerre de Jean Carré | Document from Jean Corentin Carré
text:
Jean Corentin Carré, born in Faouët (Morbihan), on January 9, 1900. On April 27, 1915, he enlisted at the age of 15 in the 41st infantry regiment. He arrived at the front at 15 years and 6 months and was assigned to the 410 infantry regiment. Section leader at 17, he earned the ranks of corporal, sergeant and adjutant, citations to the division, the army corps and the army. He died in aerial combat at the age of 18 and 3 months, on March 18, 1918. He was the youngest death for France in Faouët. In order to be able to engage, he invents an identity. His name was then Auguste Duthoy, born in Rumigny (Ardennes), on April 10, 1897. On December 29, 1916, from the depths of the trenches, he wrote a letter to Colonel Treillard, commander of the 410th infantry regiment. He asks to resume his identity, without leaving the front. This diary is kept at the Jean Carré college in Faouët, inaugurated in 1978.

title: FRBNBU-090 Amédé Fleury photographe | Amédé Fleury's story
text:
Annette Bon, contributor, comes from Marie-Madeleine Fleury (born in 1923), only daughter of Amédé Fleury (December 1887 in Lomme (Nord)-July 1966). Amédé Fleury was a teacher at the Ecole Michelet. He spent his leave at the Carmel of Compiègne in the company of his aunt. He was a sergeant in the 43rd line. As a teacher, he became a vaguemestre. He also writes for other soldiers. He has a camera with which he photographed "his" war; he develops his photographs and mentions on the back of each the dates, places, characters and events to which they refer like a logbook from 1914 to 1919. He is in the occupied zone (Lille and Lomme). Some of his photographs serve him as a support for correspondence with his wife, Marie-Madeleine Fleury.

01-Group photo (March 8, 1919) Amédé Fleury is the 2nd from the top left. 02-Photo portrait of Amédé Fleury (1915) sent to his father. 03-Photo portrait of Amédé Fleury (1916) sent to his sister, Valentine. 04-The Somme from 08/19 to 09/19/1916; Daily life in the trenches. 05-Photo "My office..." by Amédé Fleury (February 1916). 06-Photo of Alexandre Meurisse, his brother-in-law, a teacher like him, who died next to Amédé Fleury in October 1914 at the age of 20. 07-Photo of Amédé Fleury's foster family during his convalescence (September 1914). 08-1914-Beginning of the conflict. July 09, 1915 - Rosnay camp, La Meule trench. August 09, 1915 - Reims, Pontavert, Cauroy. 09-October, November 1915 - Hermonville, Sapignol bridge, Cauroy. 09-December 1915 - Cauroy, Trenches of Bois Charpentier and Luxembourg. January 10, 1916 - Le Godat, elongated wood. February 10, 1916 - La Source, Trench of Godat, Battle of Verdun. March 10, 1916 - Verdun, Sainte Catherine hospital. 10-May, June 1916 - Craonne district of the Source. July 10, 1916 - Craonnelle camps of Blanc Sablon and Beau Marais, march from Craonnelle to Epernay. August 10, 1916 - Francastel, Celestine camp; the Sum. September 10, 1916 - Battle of the Somme. October 10, 1916 - Champagne, Bois de la Chenille. 10-November 1916 - Villages of the Somme. December 10, 1916 - Cauroy, Trou Bricot. January 11, 1917 - Suippes, Montmirail, Beau Marais sector. February 11, March 1917 - Beaurieux. April 11, 1917 - Beaurieux, attack on Craonne, Plateau de Vauclerc. May 11, 1917 - La Ferté under Jouarre, Pargny Le Thus. June 11, 1917 - Mailly camp. July 11, 1917 - Courchamp, Steemwerk, Esquelberq. August 11, 1917 - The Yser, Looberghe. 11-September, October, November, December 1917 - Woesttine, Sangatte. 12-January, March, April, May, June 1918 - Clerey Chartreux, Perennes. July 12, 1918 - Croutay, Lascay, Fontenoy, Vic sur Aisne. August 12, 1918 - Auberge du chat embarrassé, Amblémy, Tartiers. September 12, 1918 - Soissons, Saconin, Breuil, Cutry, Vailly sur Aisne. 12-October, November 1918 - Colmar, Metzeral. 13-1919 - Request for news and Souvenir of Champagne. 14-Correspondence August-September 1914.

title: FRAD008_3 Documents trouvés par M. Bonalda | Documents found by M. BONALDA
text:
Mr. Bonalda found these documents in the bins of a sorting center for the most part, and in an attic for the last, between 2003 and 2004. He has no connection with the persons concerned but he has decided to keep these testimonies of the Great War for their historical value and for their link with the Ardennes. He is indeed passionate about history and a collector. In addition, some documents relate to his place of residence. He thought it was a shame to find such documents in trash cans.

Notes from Abbé BOUCHER, from Issancourt. Entry of the Germans into Mohon, by Pierre FAUVILLE. Story by Adolphe OUICE, reservist at the 247th line. Name of the Germans staying in Charleville, with their address. Response letter from the King of Spain to the request for the release of Marcel DROUART.

title: FRAD008_54 Alliance franco-américaine | Photograph from Baleux family
text: Painting representing a couple of civilians from Brieulles-sur-Bar (Ardennes) welcoming two American soldiers. It is about the arrival of the Americans in this small village in the Ardennes, at the end of 1918. Photograph acquired at a flea market by Pierre Guéry, mayor of the village of Brieulles-sur-Bar. The story could have ended there, but a few years later, the mayor receives an American couple, Mr. and Mrs. Herbert Floyd, a copy of said photo in hand. These people want to find traces of the descendants of Mr. and Mrs. Baleux, from the village who warmly welcomed the soldiers. Among these two soldiers of the American army, is Allen Floyd of the 42nd infantry division, the cousin of the father of Herbert Floyd, who was himself an infantry officer. Herbert Floyd also learns that the famous shot has a great reputation in the United States and also in France. It occupies a place of honor on the wall at the entrance to the photographic section of the National Archives in Washington and is part of a painting showing the cooperation of the two allies during the Great War of 14-18 at the Caen memorial.

title: FRAD018_105 Journal de Faidherbe GAGET | Documents from Faidherbe Gaget
text: Performing his military service in the 13th RI in Nevers, Faidherbe GAGET was 50 days from liberation when the First World War broke out. He is then a telephone operator. His diary covers the period from August 1 to October 10, 1914. Like many, he thinks the war will be short. He is however lucid and foresees that this conflict will be deadly in view of the armament of the time. He was then assigned to the 9th Engineer Regiment.

title: FRBMBX-010 Histoire de Fernand Greteau | Fernand Greteau's story
text:
Enlisted on July 24, 1914 during his military service as an aide-de-camp assigned to the care of horses in Belgium, then a stretcher bearer in the 32nd Dragoons regiment until the end of the war, my maternal grandfather Fernand Greteau was quoted at the order of his regiment in September 1914 and August 1918 for his courage and bravery. He recorded his daily memories in a series of notebooks during "his four years of nightmare" which I will publish.

1/ Postcard "In the Argonne" double-sided 2/ Mentions to the order of the regiment 3/ Postcard of the 32nd Dragoons double-sided
